did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning framework based on the EfficientNetV2 model for automated skin cancer image classification. It employs transfer learning, fine-tuning, and a Light Gradient Boosting Machine classifier on the HAM10000 dataset to classify dermoscopic images of skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing an EfficientNetV2-based deep learning framework for automated skin cancer classification. It details specific methodology including dataset preparation (HAM10000), image preprocessing, model architecture (transfer learning, Light Gradient Boosting Machine), training procedures (Adam optimizer, cross-entropy loss), and reports original results (80% accuracy, precision/recall/F1 scores). Although published in conference proceedings, it contains new empirical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: EfficientNetV2-based Deep Learning Framework for Automated Skin Cancer Image Classification
journal: Proceedings of 2025 International Conference on NexGen Networks and Cybernetics (IC2NC)
brief_summary: This study proposes an EfficientNetV2-based deep learning framework for automated skin cancer image classification using the HAM10000 dataset. The methodology involves preprocessing dermoscopic images through normalization, resizing, and augmentation, followed by transfer learning with a pretrained EfficientNetV2 model. The system is optimized using the Adam optimizer and cross-entropy loss over 10 epochs to classify various skin lesion categories. Experimental results demonstrate that the model achieves a final training accuracy of 97.89% and a weighted validation accuracy of 90%. While the model performs robustly on majority classes like nevus and dermatofibroma, it shows lower recall for minority classes such as melanoma due to class imbalance. The findings highlight the potential of EfficientNetV2 as a scalable and effective backbone for automated diagnostic support in dermatology.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion categories (benign keratosis-like lesions, melanocytic nevi, dermatofibroma, melanoma, vascular lesions, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset using an EfficientNetV2 backbone and Light Gradient Boosting Machine classifier. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels for seven lesion classes, evaluated via accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A widely recognized benchmark dataset for skin lesion analysis comprising dermoscopic images from multiple categories. It includes a metadata CSV file with image identifiers and diagnostic labels.

use.training: Used for training the EfficientNetV2-S model via stratified sampling to preserve class distribution.
use.validation: Used for validation to assess generalization and compute weighted accuracy (90%) and class-wise metrics.
use.other: Images without corresponding metadata files were excluded during preparation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To divide the HAM10000 dataset into training and validation sets while preserving the original class distribution.
To manage imbalanced datasets and improve model generalization.
For dimensionality reduction of extracted features.
To visualize correct and incorrect predictions, reveal misclassification patterns, and assess class-wise performance.
To evaluate the model's effectiveness in accurately classifying each skin lesion category, particularly important for imbalanced datasets.
To quantify overall model performance on the validation set, accounting for class imbalance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: Initial training accuracy was 74.75% with a loss of 0.7579. The model achieved consistent gains over 10 epochs. Minority classes like melanoma (mel) and actinic keratoses (akiec) showed lower recall due to class imbalance. Dermatofibroma (df) reached perfect precision (1.00) and near-perfect recall (0.96) in some textual descriptions, though Table 1 reports 0.66/0.83. Melanoma had 142 correctly identified cases, often misclassified as nevus (59) or bkl (16). Nevus samples: 1283 correctly classified, 38 misclassified as melanoma, 7 as bcc.

architecture_details.classifier_head: Fully connected layer replacing original classifier, output dimension matches number of skin lesion classes (7), followed by Softmax activation function

training_details.normalization: ImageNet dataset mean and standard deviation

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer with Softmax activation

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization of pixel values using ImageNet dataset's mean and standard deviation

Data augmentation including rotations up to 20 degrees
Exclusion of images without corresponding metadata files
Label encoding (transforming diagnostic labels into numerical indices)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training to increase data variability and reduce overfitting.
Applied up to 20 degrees to mimic real-world variations in lesion orientation.

conclusions: The proposed EfficientNetV2-based deep learning framework demonstrates robust performance for automated skin cancer image classification, achieving a weighted validation accuracy of 90% on the HAM10000 dataset.

The model effectively classifies majority classes, such as melanocytic nevi (nv) and vascular lesions (vasc), with high precision and recall, indicating strong feature learning for well-represented categories.

Class imbalance significantly impacts performance on minority classes; melanoma (mel) and actinic keratoses (akiec) exhibited lower recall (0.63–0.75) due to visual similarity with other classes and limited sample sizes.
The model achieved a final training accuracy of 97.89% and a minimal training loss of 0.1585 after 10 epochs, indicating effective learning of discriminative features from the training data.

Misclassifications primarily occurred between visually similar lesions (e.g., melanoma misclassified as nevus or benign keratosis-like lesions), highlighting the need for data balancing or augmentation for underrepresented classes.

The study highlights the potential of EfficientNetV2 as a scalable and effective backbone for automated diagnostic support in dermatology, capable of assisting clinicians in faster and more consistent diagnosis.

Future improvements should focus on addressing class imbalance through advanced augmentation techniques, synthetic sample generation (e.g., GANs or SMOTE), and incorporating clinical metadata (e.g., patient age, lesion location) to enhance robustness and reduce misclassification rates.
important_remarks: The abstract claims the framework integrates deep learning features with clinical metadata (age, sex, lesion location) and achieves 80% accuracy, but the methodology and results sections only describe image-based classification using EfficientNetV2-S and LightGBM on the HAM10000 dataset without reporting any integration of clinical metadata or the 80% accuracy figure.

There is a significant discrepancy in reported performance for the melanoma class: the abstract and conclusion mention lower recall (0.63-0.71), while the detailed results section reports precision 0.74, recall 0.64, and F1-score 0.69, and the earlier extracted data lists precision 0.89 and recall 0.63; this inconsistency requires clarification.

The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known for class imbalance (e.g., only 23 samples for dermatofibroma and 223 for melanoma), limiting the generalizability of the results to real-world clinical populations with different demographic distributions.

The model was trained for only 10 epochs, which is a very short training duration for deep learning; the authors acknowledge that longer training times could improve accuracy, suggesting the current results may be suboptimal.

No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated only on a validation split of the HAM10000 dataset, raising concerns about overfitting and lack of robustness across different data sources.

The paper does not report statistical significance tests (p-values) or confidence intervals for the reported metrics, making it difficult to assess the statistical reliability of the performance differences between classes.

The abstract mentions the use of a pretrained ResNet18 model for feature extraction in the context of integrating metadata, but the methodology specifies EfficientNetV2-S as the backbone, creating confusion about the actual architecture used for the primary classification task.
Images without corresponding metadata files were excluded during preprocessing, which may introduce selection bias if the excluded images differ systematically from those included.

The claim of '80% accuracy' in the abstract contradicts the '90% weighted validation accuracy' and '97.89% training accuracy' reported in the results, indicating potential reporting errors or different evaluation protocols not clearly defined.
